content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 26, 2018


Eric Lucan
Councilmember, City of Novato
26 Truman Drive
Novato, CA 94947

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-18-096

Dear Mr. Lucan:

       This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

       Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and that any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

       Lastly, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have
already taken place. The Commission will decline to provide advice related to conduct that has
already occurred. (Regulation 18329.)

QUESTION

(1) May you take part in decisions related to securing funding for the completion of the
Sonoma-Marin Area Rail Transit ("SMART") station in Downtown Novato (the
"Downtown Station"), which lies roughly 1,000 feet from your residential rental property?

(2) May you take part in decisions regarding the future use of the "Depot Lot," a .9-acre lot
located adjacent to the Downtown Station and roughly 800 feet from your residential rental
property?

(3) May you take part in decisions regarding the scheduling and service of the SMART train to
the Downtown Station?


¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-096
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

(1) To the extent that the decisions do not change the development density or zoning of your
property, you may take part in such decisions because the completion of the Downtown
Station will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your interests.

(2) You have not identified a specific project or decision involving the "Depot Lot." Because a
conflict of interest analysis depends on the nature of a particular decision, we cannot
generally determine whether you are disqualified from decisions involving the "Depot Lot."
We can only advise that you are disqualified from taking part in decisions related to the
"Depot Lot" that have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of your
interests.

(3) Again, as you have not identified a specific decision, we can only generally advise you that
you are disqualified from taking part in decisions related to the scheduling and service of the
SMART train to the Downtown Station that have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on any of your interests.

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR

You are a sitting member of the Novato City Council. You also serve on the SMART Board
of Directors. SMART is a regional transportation district that was established to oversee the
development and implementation of passenger rail service in Sonoma and Marin Counties.

SMART train currently services the City of Novato, with two stations located within city
limits. In January 2016, the City approved construction of a third station in an area of Downtown
Novato referred to as the "Historic Depot Site." SMART train currently passes by the Historic
Depot Site when traveling between the two stations located within the City (one of which is roughly
1.1 miles away). A "final funding plan" for the Downtown Station, which included city funds and
grants, was approved by the City in May 2017.

The final construction bids for the Downtown Station, which were recently received by the
City, exceed the funding approved in the "final funding plan." The City is now considering whether
to seek supplemental funding to meet the added costs². As a councilmember, you are expected to
participate in discussions and decisions related to securing supplemental funding should the City
choose to pursue supplemental funding.

Regardless of whether the station is completed, the SMART train will pass through
Downtown Novato potentially impacting noise levels and air quality. Once the station is complete,
engineers leaving the station would be required to make two brief horn sounds when departing.
Trains currently operate as early as 4:19 AM and as late as 9:57 PM.

Located adjacent to the "Historic Depot Site" is a .9-acre parcel referred to as the "Depot
Lot." You indicate that the City is "Beginning some planning and visioning for the site" and that

² You have stated that the City is not seeking grants or other funding mechanisms that would require changes
to the City's density, development, or zoning requirements.

File No. A-18-096
Page No. 3

you may be asked to participate. However, you are unable to identify any projects or decisions
pending at this time.

Lastly, you note that as a member of the SMART Board of Directors, you may be required
to take part in decisions regarding the scheduling and servicing of the SMART train to the
Downtown Station. However, you are unable to identify any decisions pending at this time related
to the scheduling or servicing of the SMART train to the Downtown Station.

You own a residential rental property³ in the City's historic downtown area. The rental
property, which has two units, sits approximately 1,000 feet away from the Downtown Station and
approximately 800 feet from the "Depot Lot." The City's historic downtown area is predominantly
built out, so there is minimal available space for further development.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. Under Section 87103(b), an official has a financial interest in a decision within
the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on an interest specified in Section 87103, including:

• A business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth
two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section 87103(a).)

• Real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• Any source of income aggregating $500 or more to the official within 12 months prior to
the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

You identified interests in your residential real property, your residential rental business as
both a business entity and source of income.⁴

Foreseeability and Materiality

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. (Section 87100.) The standard for
foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. An

³ You indicate that your interest in the rental property exceeds $2,000.

⁴ We note that you also have interests in your tenants as sources of income if their rent paid during the 12
months before a decision aggregates to $500 or more and may be disqualified if you know or have reason to know the
tenant has an interest in a business entity or the real property that may be foreseeably and materially affected by the
decision. (See Regulation 18702.3(a)(2).) While we conclude below that you may take part in decisions related to
securing funds for the completion of the Downtown Station, we note that you have not identified any interests held by
your tenants besides your residential rental property. To the extent that you know or have reason to know a tenant has
an interest in a business or real property (other than the rental properties you own), you should seek further advice.

File No. A-18-096
Page No. 4

interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) Based on the information you provided, your interests are not "explicitly involved" in the decisions at issue.

Where an interest is not "explicitly involved" in the decision at issue, the effect of that decision on the interest is deemed reasonably foreseeable only if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Id. at (b).)

Regulation 18702.1(b) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's financial effect on an official's interest in a business entity, including real property containing a business entity, that is not explicitly involved in the decision. (See Regulation 18702.2(a)(8).) Under Regulation 18702.1(b), a financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the effect would contribute to a change in the value of the business entity. For real property not explicitly involved in a decision, Regulation 18702.2(a) establishes that a foreseeable effect of a governmental decision on an official's financial interest is material if the decision:

"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest;

[*]...[*]

(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property."

Decisions Related to Funding the Downtown Station

Your residential rental property lies approximately 1,000 feet from the Downtown Station. Once operational, the station's ridership will increase traffic and demand for parking in the neighboring area. However, with two other stations operating within the City limits – one within 1.1 miles of the Downtown Station – those increases would appear to be limited. Moreover, in terms of parking, you note that your property includes its own dedicated parking spaces that SMART riders cannot occupy.

In regard to noise levels and air quality, the train will pass through Downtown Novato regardless of whether the Downtown Station is completed. The additional nuisance of a horn as the train leaves the station appears minimal.

Lastly, you note that your property is located in the City's historic downtown area, which is predominantly built out. Therefore, the effect on intensity of use would be limited as there is minimal available space for additional development. Additionally, there is no indication that

File No. A-18-096
Page No. 5

decisions regarding the Downtown Station, including the funding of the project, will result in a
change to the development density or zoning of your property.

While it appears that completion of the Downtown Station may have a minimal effect on
your property and business interest, to the extent that the decisions do not change the development
density or zoning of your property, we do not find that effect to be material. Therefore, based upon
the facts provided, you are permitted to take part in decisions related to securing funding for the
completion of the Downtown Station.

Decisions Related to Depot Lot and SMART Train Schedules and Services

You also requested general advice as to whether you may take part in decisions related to
the "Depot Lot" and SMART Train Schedules and Services for the Downtown Station, but did not
identify any specific decision at issue. Without further information regarding potential decisions
and their possible impacts on your interests, we can only advise you generally that you are
disqualified from taking part in decisions related to the "Depot Lot" and SMART Train Schedules
and Services at the Downtown Station that have a foreseeable and material effect on any interests
you have, such as your residential rental property and rental business.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Acting General Counsel

By: Adam E. Silver
Counsel, Legal Division

AES:jgl

property? 
(3) May you take part in decisions regarding the scheduling and service of the SMART train to 
the Downtown Station?

CONCLUSION: (1) To the extent that the decisions do not change the development density or zoning of your 
property, you may take part in such decisions because the completion of the Downtown 
Station will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your interests. 
(2) You have not identified a specific project or decision involving the "Depot Lot." Because a 
conflict of interest analysis depends on the nature of a particular decision, we cannot 
generally determine whether you are disqualified from decisions involving the "Depot Lot." 
We can only advise that you are disqualified from taking part in decisions related to the

effec~ on any of your interests.

to the City's density, development, or zoning requirements.
